LAW AND MOTION, DEPT. 302, THE TENTATIVE RULING IS ADOPTED ON PLAINTIFF MARIA GONZALEZ'S MOTION FOR ORDER COMPELLING ARBITRATION AS FOLLOWS: PLAINTIFF MARIA DEL CARMEN GONZALEZ'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION, IN WHICH DEFENDANT MACAVINI, INC. JOINED, IS DENIED. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING.) COUNSEL FOR THE BROKER DEFENDANTS IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS THE COURT'S RULING VERBATIM AND COMPLIES WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: GINA GONZALES; NOT REPORTED. (302/EPS)

MASTER COURT CALENDAR SET FOR SEP-14-2020 CONTINUED TO MASTER CALENDAR COURT ON APR-05-2021 AT 9:30 AM IN DEPT. 206. RAMIRO CASTRO, ESQ. APPEARING FOR PLAINTIFF VIA COURTCALL. ROGER HONEY, ESQ. APPEARING FOR DEFENDANTS MONIQUE HARITCHABALET AND FUTURA REALTY, INC. DBA RE/MAX FUTURA VIA PHONE. THE COURT GRANTS A CONTINUANCE OF TRIAL TO 4/5/21 AT 9:30AM IN DEPT. 206 ON THE CONDITION THAT A FILING FEE OF $60.00 MUST BE PAID VIA E-FILING WITH A COVER LETTER. THE COURT FURTHER ORDERS THAT DISCOVERY IS CLOSED. IF THERE'S A STAY IN THE LITIGATION, PARTIES MAY FILE A MOTION WITH DEPT. 302. ALL PARTIES ARE ORDERED TO BE APPEAR PHYSICALLY ON 4/5/21. CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-26-2019 Continued To Nov-13-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Petition(Motion) For Order Compelling Arbitration: Notice Of Hearing Thereon
Off Calendar Aug-22-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance, the tentative ruling is adopted on PLAINTIFF MARIA GONZALEZ'S Motion For Order Compelling Arbitration as follows:

Plaintiff Maria Del Carmen Gonzalez's motion to compel arbitration, in which defendant Macavini, Inc. joined, is denied. The general rule is that non-parties to an arbitration agreement may not be compelled to arbitrate disputes under the agreement. (See Suh v. Superior Court (2010) 181 Cal.App.4th 1504, 1512 ["Even the strong public policy in favor of arbitration does not extend to those who are not parties to an arbitration agreement or who have not authorized anyone to act for them in executing such an agreement."].) While there are a number of theories that serve as exceptions to this general rule (see id. at 1513), plaintiff has failed to show that any of those exceptions applies to the broker defendants.

A nonsignatory can be compelled to arbitrate on the basis of an agency relation between the signatory and the nonsignatory when the relationship "makes it equitable to impose the duty to arbitrate upon the non-signatory." (Westra v. Marcus & Millichap Real Estate Investment Brokerage Co., Inc. (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 759, 765.) However, a showing of agency relationship does not suffice; rather, the court must also examine "whether that preexisting relationship is of such a nature that it supports a finding of 'implied authority for [one of the signatories] to bind [the nonsignatory] by their arbitration agreement." (Jensen v. U-Haul Co. of California (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 295, 303.) It may be equitable for "a signatory to compel a nonsignatory to arbitrate so long as another signatory had authority to bind the nonsignatory to the arbitration agreement. [Citation.] The requirements for imposing arbitration on a nonsignatory principal, as opposed to allowing a nonsignatory agent to compel arbitration, however, must be 'exacting.'" (Cohen v. TNP 2008 Participating Notes Program, LLC (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 840, 864.) Here, unlike in Westra v. Marcus & Millichap Real Estate Investment Brokerage Co., Inc. (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 759, plaintiff and defendant are seeking to compel the nonsignatory broker defendants to arbitrate. "It is one thing to permit a nonsignatory to relinquish his right to a jury trial, but quite another to compel him to do so." (Benasra v. Marciano (2001) 92 Cal.App.4th 987, 991.)

Most importantly, the language of the agreement explicitly states that "Brokers are not Parties to this Agreement"; provides that the Parties "agree to mediate any disputes or claims with Broker(s) who, in writing, agree to such mediation prior to, or within a reasonable time after, the dispute or claim is presented to the Broker"; and, critically, that "[B]rokers shall not be obligated or compelled to mediate or arbitrate unless they agree so in writing." (Agreement Paras. 1(F), 30 (B) and (C) (3).) Thus, the agreement expressly contradicts the notion that the brokers may be compelled to arbitrate claims against them unless they agree to do so in writing, whether on an agency or other theory.

Counsel for the broker defendants is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats the court's ruling verbatim and complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.
